questions.stem:
What does this code print?

`int x = 7;`
`if (x > 5) printf("big"); else printf("small");`
questions.explanation: The condition `x > 5` is `7 > 5`, which is true, so the `if` branch runs and prints `big`. The `else` branch is skipped because only one of the two branches executes.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
How many times does the body of this loop execute?

`for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { printf("*"); }`
questions.explanation: The loop runs for `i` equal to `0, 1, 2, 3, 4`, which is five iterations. It stops when `i` becomes `5` because `5 < 5` is false. So `*` is printed five times.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this loop print?

`int sum = 0;`
`for (int i = 1; i <= 4; i++) sum += i;`
`printf("%d", sum);`
questions.explanation: The loop adds `1 + 2 + 3 + 4` into `sum`. That total is `10`. The loop runs while `i <= 4`, so it includes `i = 4`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does the `break` statement do inside a loop?
options: Restarts the loop from the beginning
Pauses the program until a key is pressed
Skips the rest of the current iteration and continues with the next one
questions.explanation: `break` terminates the innermost loop (or `switch`) immediately and transfers control to the statement after it. The option that skips only the current iteration describes `continue`, not `break`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does the `continue` statement do inside a loop?
options: Skips the remaining statements in the current iteration and jumps to the next iteration
questions.explanation: `continue` abandons the rest of the current iteration's body and proceeds to the loop's next iteration (re-evaluating the condition, and running the update part in a `for` loop). Exiting the loop entirely is what `break` does.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this code print?

`for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { if (i == 2) continue; printf("%d", i); }`
questions.explanation: When `i` is `2`, `continue` skips the `printf` for that iteration only, so `2` is not printed. Every other value (`0, 1, 3, 4`) is printed, giving `0134`. The loop does not stop, so it is not `01`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this code print?

`for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { if (i == 3) break; printf("%d", i); }`
questions.explanation: The loop prints `0`, `1`, `2`, then when `i` reaches `3` the `break` exits the loop before printing, so `3` and `4` never appear. The output is `012`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
How many times does this `while` loop run its body?

`int i = 0;`
`while (i < 3) { printf("x"); i++; }`
questions.explanation: The body runs for `i` equal to `0, 1, 2`, which is three times. After the third run `i` becomes `3`, and `3 < 3` is false, so the loop stops. Because `i++` is present, it is not infinite.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What is the key difference between a `while` loop and a `do-while` loop in C?
options: `do-while` always executes its body at least once, even if the condition is false
questions.explanation: A `do-while` checks its condition after running the body, so the body always executes at least once. A `while` checks the condition first, so its body may run zero times if the condition is false initially.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
How many times does this `do-while` loop print, given the condition is false at the start?

`int i = 10;`
`do { printf("hi"); } while (i < 5);`
questions.explanation: A `do-while` runs the body before testing, so `hi` is printed once. Then `10 < 5` is false, so the loop ends. A plain `while` with the same condition would have printed nothing.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
Given `int n = 2;`, what does this `switch` print?

`switch (n) { case 1: printf("one"); break; case 2: printf("two"); break; default: printf("other"); }`
questions.explanation: The `switch` jumps to `case 2`, prints `two`, and the `break` then exits the `switch` so `default` is not reached. The output is `two`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
Given `int n = 1;`, what does this `switch` print? (Notice there is no `break` after `case 1`.)

`switch (n) { case 1: printf("A"); case 2: printf("B"); break; default: printf("C"); }`
questions.explanation: With no `break` after `case 1`, execution falls through into `case 2`, so both `A` and `B` print. The `break` after `B` then stops the fall-through before `default`. The output is `AB`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this code print?

`int x = 4;`
`if (x % 2 == 0) printf("even"); else printf("odd");`
questions.explanation: `x % 2` is `4 % 2`, which is `0`. The test `0 == 0` is true, so the `if` branch runs and prints `even`. This is the standard way to test for an even number.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What is wrong with this condition meant to test equality?

`if (x = 5) printf("yes");`
options: Nothing, it correctly tests whether `x` equals `5`
It assigns `5` to `x` instead of comparing, so the condition is always true
It is a syntax error that will not compile
questions.explanation: A single `=` is assignment, so `x = 5` stores `5` in `x` and the condition evaluates to `5`, which is nonzero and therefore true every time. The equality comparison requires `==`. This is a classic C bug that compiles without error.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this nested loop print in total (the number of `#` characters)?

`for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) for (int j = 0; j < 2; j++) printf("#");`
questions.explanation: The inner loop runs `2` times for each of the `3` outer iterations, so the body runs `3 * 2 = 6` times, printing six `#` characters.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which loop is most natural when you know in advance exactly how many times to repeat?
questions.explanation: A `for` loop puts the initialization, the stopping condition, and the update in one place, which fits counting a known number of iterations. A `while` works too, but the counted form is idiomatic with `for`. An `if` and a `switch` do not repeat at all.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this code print?

`int i = 5;`
`while (i > 0) { printf("%d", i); i--; }`
questions.explanation: Starting at `5` and decrementing, the loop prints `5`, `4`, `3`, `2`, `1`. When `i` reaches `0` the test `0 > 0` is false, so `0` is not printed. The output is `54321`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
Given `int score = 75;`, which branch runs?

`if (score >= 80) printf("A"); else if (score >= 70) printf("B"); else printf("C");`
questions.explanation: `score >= 80` is `75 >= 80`, which is false, so it checks the next condition `score >= 70`, which is `75 >= 70`, true. It prints `B` and skips the rest of the chain. Only one branch in an if-else-if ladder runs.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this loop compute in `p`?

`int p = 1;`
`for (int i = 1; i <= 4; i++) p *= i;`
`printf("%d", p);`
questions.explanation: The loop multiplies `1 * 1 * 2 * 3 * 4`, which is the factorial of `4`, equal to `24`. Starting `p` at `1` is essential so the first multiplication keeps the value correct.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: In C, what counts as a false condition for `if` and loops?
options: The integer value `0`; any nonzero value is true
questions.explanation: C treats the integer `0` as false and every nonzero value (including negative numbers) as true. There is no separate boolean requirement in classic C, so an expression like `if (3)` is always true.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this code print?

`int count = 0;`
`for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) { if (i % 2 == 0) count++; }`
`printf("%d", count);`
questions.explanation: The loop increments `count` only when `i` is even. Between `1` and `10` the even numbers are `2, 4, 6, 8, 10`, which is five values, so `count` ends at `5`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
Why does this loop never terminate?

`int i = 0;`
`while (i < 5) { printf("loop"); }`
options: `i` is never changed inside the loop, so `i < 5` stays true forever
questions.explanation: There is no `i++` or other update to `i` inside the loop, so `i` remains `0` and the condition `i < 5` is always true. The loop runs forever. Adding `i++;` inside the body fixes it.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Why is `break` important inside the cases of a `switch` statement?
options: It prints the matched case label
It is required syntax or the program will not compile
Without it, execution falls through into the following cases
It converts the `switch` into a loop
questions.explanation: After a matching `case` runs, execution continues into the next case unless a `break` stops it. This fall-through behavior is sometimes wanted but usually a bug, so each case typically ends with `break`. It is not a compile requirement; omitting it is legal but changes behavior.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Trace a counting loop and a remainder test.
questions.solution: Consider `int sum = 0; for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) sum += i;`. After the loop, `sum` holds {{1}}. The loop body executed {{2}} times. Separately, the boolean expression `7 % 2 == 0` evaluates to the integer {{3}} because `7` is odd.
questions.explanation: Summing `1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5` gives `15`, and the loop ran once for each value from `1` to `5`, so five iterations. Since `7 % 2` is `1`, the test `1 == 0` is false, which in C is the integer `0`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Choose the right control-flow keyword for each behavior.
questions.solution: The statement that immediately exits the innermost loop is {{1}}. The statement that skips to the next iteration without leaving the loop is {{2}}. The loop construct that is guaranteed to run its body at least once is the {{3}} loop.
questions.explanation: `break` leaves the loop entirely; `continue` jumps to the next iteration; and `do-while` tests its condition after the body, so the body always runs at least once.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Predict the output of a loop containing a continue.
questions.solution: For `for (int i = 0; i < 6; i++) { if (i == 4) continue; printf("%d", i); }`, the digit that is skipped is {{1}}, so the total number of digits printed is {{2}}. The loop variable reaches a final value of {{3}} just before the condition `i < 6` becomes false.
questions.explanation: When `i` is `4`, `continue` skips the print, so `4` is omitted. The values `0, 1, 2, 3, 5` are printed, which is five digits. The last value that satisfies `i < 6` is `5`; after the update `i` becomes `6` and the loop stops.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Trace a switch with a deliberate fall-through.
questions.solution: Given `int n = 1;` and `switch (n) { case 1: printf("X"); case 2: printf("Y"); break; default: printf("Z"); }`, the program prints the letters in order: first {{1}}, then {{2}}, and the total number of letters printed is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Matching `case 1` prints `X`, and with no `break` there it falls through into `case 2` and prints `Y`. The `break` after `Y` stops execution before `default`, so two letters are printed.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Count iterations across loop forms.
questions.solution: The loop `for (int i = 2; i <= 10; i += 2) {...}` executes its body {{1}} times. The loop `while (i < 0) {...}` starting with `i = 3` executes its body {{2}} times. The loop `do {...} while (i < 0);` starting with `i = 3` executes its body {{3}} times.
questions.explanation: Stepping by `2` from `2` to `10` gives `2, 4, 6, 8, 10`, which is five iterations. A `while` whose condition is false at the start runs zero times. A `do-while` runs its body once before testing, even when the condition is false.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Reason about C truth values and equality.
questions.solution: In C, the only integer value treated as false is {{1}}. The condition `if (x = 0)` (note the single equals) is always {{2}} because assignment yields the assigned value. The correct operator for testing equality in a condition is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Only `0` is false in C; every other integer is true. The expression `x = 0` assigns `0` and evaluates to `0`, which is false, so that condition is always false. Equality is tested with `==`, while `=` is assignment.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Trace a nested loop count and a factorial accumulation.
questions.solution: The nested loop `for (i = 0; i < 4; i++) for (j = 0; j < 3; j++) printf("*");` prints {{1}} stars in total. Separately, the loop `int p = 1; for (int k = 1; k <= 5; k++) p *= k;` leaves `p` equal to {{2}}, which is the factorial of {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The inner loop runs `3` times for each of the `4` outer passes, so `4 * 3 = 12` stars. Multiplying `1 * 2 * 3 * 4 * 5` gives `120`, the factorial of `5`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Read three integers and print the largest of the three.
questions.explanation: Start with the first number as the largest so far, then compare it with the other two using `if`, updating when you find something bigger.
questions.source: in the style of the KNUST Scientific Computing course (Introduction to C, MATH 279 / HPC)

questions.stem: Read an integer and print the word even if it is even, otherwise print odd.
questions.explanation: The remainder operator `%` tells you if a number is even: `n % 2` is 0 for even numbers. Zero counts as even.
questions.source: in the style of the KNUST Scientific Computing course (Introduction to C, MATH 279 / HPC)

questions.stem: Read an integer score and print its grade: A for 70 and above, B for 60 to 69, C for 50 to 59, D for 40 to 49, otherwise F.
questions.explanation: An if/else if ladder checks the bands from the top down. A score of 55 falls past A and B and lands on C.
questions.source: in the style of the KNUST Scientific Computing course (Introduction to C, MATH 279 / HPC)

questions.stem: Read a positive integer n and print the sum 1 + 2 + ... + n.
questions.explanation: A count-controlled `for` loop adds every integer from 1 to n. Using a long for the running total leaves room for large sums. For n = 100 the total is 5050.
questions.source: in the style of the KNUST Scientific Computing course (Introduction to C, MATH 279 / HPC)

questions.stem: Read two integers, a base and an exponent, and print the base raised to that power.
questions.explanation: Start at 1 and multiply by the base exponent times with a count-controlled loop. Any base to the power 0 is 1, since the loop runs zero times.
questions.source: in the style of the KNUST Scientific Computing course (Introduction to C, MATH 279 / HPC)

questions.stem: Read an integer n and print a right triangle of n rows, where row i contains i asterisks.
questions.explanation: An outer loop counts the rows and an inner loop prints that many asterisks, followed by a newline. Nested loops like this build up two-dimensional patterns.
questions.source: in the style of the KNUST Scientific Computing course (Introduction to C, MATH 279 / HPC)

questions.stem: Read an integer n and print how many prime numbers are less than or equal to n.
questions.explanation: For each number up to n, test whether any divisor from 2 to its square root divides it. Count the ones with no divisor. Up to 10 the primes are 2, 3, 5, 7.
questions.source: in the style of the KNUST Scientific Computing course (Introduction to C, MATH 279 / HPC)
